content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 5, 2003

David R. Zamora
296 W. Van Buren Street
Colton, CA 92324

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-03-267

Dear Mr. Zamora:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

Must office space donated to a candidate be disclosed as a non-monetary contribution?

CONCLUSION

Yes. The donated office space is a non-monetary contribution reported on Schedule C of the campaign disclosure form and valued at a comparable rental rate for similar office space in the area.

FACTS

In our November 24, 2003, telephone conversation, you confirmed that you are a candidate for the San Bernardino County Board of Supervisors. You have been offered the use of office space for your campaign and are asking if it is permissible to receive, and if so, how would it be reported.

___________________________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-03-267
Page No. 2

ANALYSIS

Under the Act, "a contribution" is any payment made for political purposes
without full and adequate consideration to the donor. (Section 82015; regulation
18215.) A payment is made for political purposes if it is received by or made at the behest of a
candidate, unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that the payment was
made for purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy for elective office. (Section
82015(b)(2)(B).) A payment is made at the behest of a candidate it if is made in
cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with the candidate. (Regulation
18225.7.)

The Act's definition of "contribution" also includes "in-kind" or nonmonetary
contributions. Nonmonetary or in-kind contributions are goods and services provided
free of charge to a candidate or committee, and goods and services provided at a discount
from the fair market value to a candidate or committee (unless the same discount is given
in the regular course of business to members of the public). (Section 82015(c);
regulation 18215(b)(3).)

Under the Act, a candidate is not prohibited from receiving a contribution in the
form of office space. A candidate receiving a non-monetary contribution must disclose
the fair market value of the contribution. The fair market value is the price at which the
candidate could obtain the goods or services on the open market. (Section 82025.5;
Semaniak Advice Letter, No. A-03-092.) For example, the value of office space may be
computed based on comparable rental rates for office space in the area. You must report
in-kind contributions received on Schedule C of the Form 460.

I trust this answers your question sufficiently. If you have any other questions
regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

by: Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division

CONCLUSION: Yes. The donated office space is a non-monetary contribution reported on
